title: Hospital Financial Counselor: How to Use This Free Resource Before Your Bill Arrives
excerpt: Most patients never know a hospital financial counselor exists until collections calls start. Learn what these specialists can do for you, what documents to bring, and the exact questions that unlock the biggest reductions.
metaDescription: Learn what a hospital financial counselor does, how to request a meeting before your bill arrives, and what to ask to unlock charity care and hardship waivers.

content.text: There is a person on staff at nearly every hospital in the United States whose entire job is to help you pay less. Their title varies — hospital financial counselor, patient financial services specialist, financial assistance counselor — but their function is the same: they sit between you and a catastrophic bill, armed with discount programs, charity care applications, and hardship waivers that most patients never learn exist. The tragedy is that most people only discover this resource after they've already received a bill they can't pay, missed the prompt-pay discount window, or worse, been sent to collections.

This guide will show you exactly how to find a hospital financial counselor before your bill is generated, what documents to bring to that meeting, and the specific questions that unlock the most significant reductions. Whether you're facing a planned procedure or you're in the middle of an unexpected hospitalization, engaging this resource proactively can mean the difference between a $15,000 bill and a $1,500 one — or no bill at all.
What a Hospital Financial Counselor Actually Does

The common misconception is that hospital financial counselors only set up payment plans. That's the least powerful thing they can do for you. A trained hospital financial assistance counselor has access to a range of programs that most patients never ask about. Understanding the full scope of their authority is the first step to getting real results.

Charity care application processing is one of the most impactful tools at their disposal. Under IRS Section 501(r), every nonprofit hospital is legally required to have a written financial assistance policy (FAP) and to screen patients for eligibility. Nonprofit hospitals represent about 58% of all U.S. community hospitals. This isn't a discretionary program — it's a federal mandate. A hospital financial counselor is the person who initiates that screening process and processes your application. If you never ask to see them, that screening may never happen.

Beyond charity care, financial counselors can apply prompt-pay discounts (typically 10–30% for payment within 30 days), initiate itemized bill reviews to catch errors before a final bill is generated, submit requests for hardship waivers that reduce or eliminate balances after insurance has paid, and connect you with external assistance programs like state Medicaid, pharmaceutical assistance programs, and disease-specific nonprofits. Some hospital financial counselors can also negotiate directly toward a discounted lump-sum settlement — the same outcome a billing advocate might charge 25–35% of savings to achieve.

content.title: Key Takeaway
content.text: A hospital financial counselor is not just a payment plan coordinator. They are your gateway to charity care, hardship waivers, prompt-pay discounts, and itemized bill audits. Engaging them proactively — ideally before your bill is finalized — gives you the most options and the most leverage.
When to Request a Meeting: Before the Bill Is Generated

Timing matters enormously with hospital financial counselors. Most patients make the mistake of waiting until they receive a bill in the mail, reviewing the sticker shock total, and then calling to ask about options. By that point, several deadlines may have already passed. Prompt-pay discount windows are typically 30 days from the date of service or from the date the bill is generated. Some charity care programs have application deadlines as short as 240 days from the date of service (the minimum required by the ACA). Applying before the bill is finalized gives you the most leverage.

The ideal time to request a hospital financial counselor meeting is in three specific situations. First, before a scheduled non-emergency procedure — once you receive a scheduled date for surgery, an inpatient procedure, or even a significant diagnostic test, contact the hospital's billing department or patient financial services office immediately. Ask them to connect you with a financial counselor before the procedure so you can discuss your options and get a Good Faith Estimate under the No Surprises Act.

Second, during an extended hospitalization — if you're admitted to the hospital, especially for an unplanned stay, the hospital is actually required under the ACA to make reasonable efforts to determine your eligibility for financial assistance and to notify you about available programs. Ask your nurse or patient advocate to connect you with a hospital billing department representative or financial counselor during your stay.

Third, within the first week after discharge — even if you didn't connect with a counselor beforehand, reaching out immediately after discharge keeps all options open. The prompt-pay window is still intact, the charity care application window is wide open, and no collections activity has begun. Most hospitals have dedicated financial counseling offices. Call the main hospital number and ask for 'patient financial services' or 'financial assistance' to be connected.
Documents to Bring to Your Financial Counselor Meeting

Walking into a hospital financial counselor meeting unprepared is one of the most common mistakes patients make. The counselor's ability to help you depends almost entirely on the documentation you provide. Programs are income-based, and the counselor needs to verify your financial situation to approve any significant reductions. Bring more documentation than you think you need — it is far better to have it and not need it than to have to reschedule because you're missing a key form.
Federal Tax Returns (Most Recent 1–2 Years)

Your most recent federal income tax return (Form 1040) is the single most important document you can bring. It provides a verified snapshot of your annual income and household size — the two primary factors that determine charity care and hardship waiver eligibility. Bring the full return, not just the first page. Some hospitals will want to see Schedule C if you're self-employed, or Schedule D if you have capital gains income.
Recent Pay Stubs (Last 2–3 Months)

Pay stubs document your current income, which may differ significantly from what's shown on your tax return — especially if you recently lost income, switched jobs, or had a reduction in hours. If your current income is lower than your last tax return shows, the pay stubs are actually more helpful to your case. Bring pay stubs for all household members with income. If you're paid irregularly or are self-employed, bring bank statements covering the last three months instead.
Bank Statements (Last 2–3 Months)

Bank statements verify your liquid assets, which some hospitals factor into hardship determinations. They also help if you're self-employed and don't have traditional pay stubs. Some programs exclude certain assets like retirement accounts or home equity from their calculations — ask the counselor specifically how assets are evaluated under their policy.
Insurance Cards and Explanation of Benefits (EOB)

If you have insurance, bring your insurance card and any Explanation of Benefits documents you've received related to this visit. The financial counselor needs to understand what insurance has paid or will pay before calculating your patient responsibility. If you received care before insurance was applied, the counselor can often hold the account while claims are processed.
Documentation of Extraordinary Expenses

Many hardship programs allow for the deduction of extraordinary ongoing expenses from your income calculation. These might include ongoing medical expenses for other family members, high housing costs relative to income, childcare costs, elder care costs, or recent catastrophic events. Document these with statements, receipts, or letters. The more complete your picture of financial hardship, the more a financial counselor can do for you.

links.text: How to Prove Financial Hardship to Your Hospital
How Hospital Charity Care Programs Work
How to Get an Itemized Medical Bill

content.text: The Exact Questions to Ask Your Hospital Financial Counselor

Most patients in a financial counselor meeting answer questions but don't ask them. That's a missed opportunity. The counselor's job is to process your application for programs that exist — they may not proactively mention every option unless you ask. These are the specific questions that consistently surface the most powerful outcomes.

items: What is your hospital's financial assistance policy, and can I have a copy of it? (Under IRS 501(r) rules, nonprofit hospitals must provide this on request.)
What is the income threshold for 100% charity care at this hospital? (Many hospitals cover 100% of charges for patients at or below 200% of the Federal Poverty Level.)
What income range qualifies for partial reductions, and what are those reduction percentages? (Many hospitals have sliding scales up to 300–400% FPL.)
Do you offer a prompt-pay discount if I can pay a settled amount today or within 30 days?
Can I get an itemized bill reviewed before the final statement is generated to identify any billing errors?
Does your hospital participate in any state or county financial assistance programs I should apply for separately?
Is there a medical debt hardship waiver program separate from the charity care program?
If I don't qualify for charity care, what is the lowest settlement you can offer on this account?
If the bill has already been generated, is there a time limit on applying for financial assistance?
Who specifically do I follow up with, and what is the best direct contact number to reach you?

content.text: Write down the name of the counselor you meet with and every commitment they make. Request a confirmation in writing whenever a program application is submitted or a discount is approved. The hospital billing department handles hundreds of accounts — verbal commitments can disappear without a paper trail.
What Outcomes Are Realistic

Understanding realistic outcomes helps you evaluate whether a meeting with a hospital financial counselor is achieving what it should. The range of outcomes is wide, and it depends heavily on your income relative to the Federal Poverty Level (FPL), the hospital's specific financial assistance policy, and the size of the bill.

For patients at or below 100% FPL — the federal poverty level for a single person is $15,060 in 2025 — complete bill forgiveness is common at nonprofit hospitals. The ACA requires nonprofit hospitals to provide free or discounted care to patients who qualify under their financial assistance policy, and most policies set 100% forgiveness at the 100–200% FPL threshold. A family of four at or below approximately $31,200 in annual income would typically qualify at most hospitals.

For patients at 200–300% FPL, partial reductions of 50–80% are realistic at hospitals with robust financial assistance programs. At 300–400% FPL, reductions of 20–50% are achievable through a combination of charity care, prompt-pay discounts, and hardship adjustments. For patients above 400% FPL with very large bills — say, $50,000 or more — a negotiated lump-sum settlement at a significant discount from the patient responsibility amount is still achievable, even if standard charity care programs don't apply.

Prompt-pay discounts are available regardless of income in many cases. If the financial counselor tells you the charity care application is under review and may take two to four weeks to process, ask whether you can lock in a prompt-pay discount rate now that will apply to whatever balance remains after the charity care determination. Some hospitals will do this.
When the Hospital Says No: Your Next Steps

A flat 'no' from a hospital financial counselor is rarely the final word — it usually means you've hit the limits of what that particular person can approve, not the limits of what the hospital can do. There are several productive responses to a denial.

First, ask for the reason in writing. Under IRS 501(r) requirements, nonprofit hospitals must provide written notice of financial assistance determinations. If you're denied, ask for the specific reason so you can address it or appeal. Sometimes a denial is based on incomplete documentation — providing a missing pay stub or clarifying your household size is enough to reverse it.

Second, ask to speak with a supervisor in patient financial services. Front-line counselors often have limited approval authority. A supervisor or director of patient financial services can approve exceptions that a standard counselor cannot. Third, ask about the hospital's formal appeals process. Most nonprofit hospitals have one, and it exists specifically for situations where a patient believes a financial assistance denial was incorrect.

If those avenues are exhausted, shift to direct negotiation for a lump-sum settlement. Even patients who don't qualify for formal assistance programs can often negotiate a meaningful discount — sometimes 30–50% — if they can offer a lump-sum payment. A hospital billing department that cannot offer charity care may still have authority to settle an account at a reduced amount rather than send it to collections, where the hospital will typically recover only 20–40 cents on the dollar.

links.text: How to Negotiate Medical Bills: The Complete Guide
How to Dispute a Medical Bill Step by Step

content.text: The Difference Between Hospital Financial Counselors and Patient Advocates

It's worth clarifying the distinction between a hospital financial counselor and a patient advocate, because patients sometimes conflate the two roles. A hospital financial counselor works for the hospital. Their job is to help you access the hospital's own financial assistance programs and to set up payment arrangements that work for both parties. They can do a great deal, but they ultimately represent the hospital's interests alongside yours.

A patient advocate — either a hospital-employed patient representative or an independent medical billing advocate you hire — represents your interests specifically. An independent advocate audits your bill for errors, negotiates on your behalf, and may be more aggressive in pushing for reductions beyond what the hospital's programs formally offer. For bills under $5,000, a hospital financial counselor is usually your best first stop. For larger, more complex bills, a counselor meeting is the starting point, but you may benefit from bringing in additional support.
Specific Income Thresholds to Know Before Your Meeting

Coming into a financial counselor meeting with a clear understanding of Federal Poverty Level thresholds puts you in a stronger position. The 2025 Federal Poverty Level for a single person is $15,060 annually. For a family of four, it is $31,200. These figures are updated annually by the Department of Health and Human Services.

When hospitals refer to income thresholds in their financial assistance policies, they typically express them as a percentage of FPL. A policy that covers patients at or below 200% FPL for full forgiveness covers a single person earning up to $30,120 or a family of four earning up to $62,400. A policy with a sliding scale up to 400% FPL means partial discounts extend to a single person earning up to $60,240 or a family of four earning up to $124,800. Knowing these numbers before you walk in lets you have a more informed conversation about what you likely qualify for.

You can look up your hospital's specific financial assistance policy before your meeting. The ACA requires nonprofit hospitals to post their financial assistance policy, application form, and plain-language summary on their website and to make physical copies available in the facility. Reviewing the policy in advance tells you the exact income thresholds, required documentation, and application deadlines — which gives you a significant advantage in the counselor meeting.

links.text: Common Hospital Billing Errors and How to Catch Them
Medical Debt Forgiveness Programs That Can Erase Your Balance

content.text: Making the Most of Your Meeting: Practical Tips

items: Request the meeting in writing (email) so you have a record that you initiated the financial assistance process.
Bring two copies of every document — one for them, one for you to keep.
Take notes during the meeting, including the counselor's full name, title, direct phone number, and the reference number for any applications submitted.
Ask what the timeline is for a decision and what the next steps are if you don't hear back.
Follow up in writing after the meeting with a brief summary of what was discussed and what was applied for.
Do not make any payments on the account while a financial assistance application is pending — payments can be interpreted as acceptance of the billed amount.
If you're denied, ask immediately about the appeals process before you leave the meeting.

content.text: The hospital financial assistance counselor system is one of the most underused consumer protections in American healthcare. Millions of patients pay bills they could have had dramatically reduced or eliminated simply because they didn't know this resource existed or didn't engage it proactively. The knowledge you've just read is the starting point — applying it is what changes your outcome.

title: How to Get a Medical Bill Waived: Income-Based Programs and Hardship Strategies
excerpt: A medical bill waived to zero is not a fantasy — it's the formal outcome of programs that most hospitals are legally required to offer. Here's how to trigger the process, what qualifies as a hardship, and what to say when the hospital pushes back.
metaDescription: Learn how to get a medical bill waived or reduced to zero using charity care, hardship waivers, and prompt-pay discounts. Step-by-step guide with income thresholds.

content.text: Getting a medical bill waived — reduced to zero — is not a fantasy or a lucky exception. It is the formally documented outcome of programs that the majority of U.S. hospitals are legally required to maintain. The process has a name (charity care or financial assistance), a federal mandate behind it (IRS Section 501(r) and the Affordable Care Act), and specific income thresholds that determine eligibility. Millions of Americans qualify and never claim these benefits, paying thousands of dollars on bills that could have been eliminated.

This guide covers exactly how to get a medical bill waived or significantly reduced: the income-based programs that can bring a bill to zero, what 'hardship' actually means in both hospital policy and IRS rules, how to activate the process, and the specific language and strategies that work when a hospital initially says no.
Why Hospitals Can and Do Waive Medical Bills

Understanding the financial and legal structure behind medical bill waivers helps you engage the process with confidence rather than desperation. About 58% of U.S. community hospitals are nonprofit organizations. To maintain their federal tax-exempt status under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, these hospitals must satisfy requirements codified in IRS Section 501(r), which was added by the Affordable Care Act.

One of those 501(r) requirements is that every nonprofit hospital must establish and maintain a written financial assistance policy (FAP) — commonly called a charity care policy. The FAP must specify eligibility criteria, the method for applying for assistance, how the hospital identifies patients who may qualify, and the basis for calculating any discounts. Hospitals that fail to comply risk losing their tax-exempt status — a consequence so financially significant that compliance is essentially universal.

For-profit hospitals are not subject to 501(r) requirements, but many maintain voluntary financial assistance programs anyway — competitive pressure, state regulations, and the practical reality that collecting on a bill that goes to collections yields only 20–40 cents on the dollar all incentivize offering discounts to patients who ask. The programs may be less generous or consistent than at nonprofit hospitals, but they often exist.

content.title: Key Takeaway
content.text: Nonprofit hospitals — 58% of all U.S. community hospitals — are legally required under IRS 501(r) to maintain charity care programs. Getting a medical bill waived is not asking for a favor; it is activating a federally mandated benefit you are entitled to claim.
Income Thresholds: Who Qualifies for 100% Bill Forgiveness

Charity care programs use Federal Poverty Level (FPL) thresholds to determine eligibility. The Department of Health and Human Services updates these thresholds annually. For 2025, the FPL is $15,060 for a single person, $20,440 for a two-person household, $25,820 for a family of three, and $31,200 for a family of four, with $5,380 added for each additional household member.

The majority of nonprofit hospitals offer 100% charity care — meaning your bill is waived entirely — to patients whose income falls at or below 200% FPL. That means a single person earning up to $30,120 or a family of four earning up to $62,400 would qualify for complete bill forgiveness at most nonprofit hospitals. Some hospital systems are more generous: Johns Hopkins, for example, provides 100% financial assistance to patients at or below 300% FPL. The specific threshold varies by hospital, which is why looking up your hospital's actual financial assistance policy before you apply is worthwhile.

Beyond the 100% forgiveness threshold, most hospitals have sliding-scale programs that extend partial reductions to patients at higher income levels. A typical sliding scale might provide 75% reduction for patients at 200–250% FPL, 50% reduction at 250–300% FPL, 25% reduction at 300–350% FPL, and no formal discount above 350–400% FPL. Under the ACA, the 501(r) rules also prohibit nonprofit hospitals from charging more than the 'amounts generally billed' (AGB) — which is typically the Medicare rate or insurance-negotiated rate — to patients who qualify for financial assistance but pay out-of-pocket.
How to Trigger the Charity Care Review Process

Triggering the charity care review process is more deliberate than most people realize. The hospital will not automatically assess your eligibility just because you received care there. You need to take specific actions to initiate the process.

items: Contact the hospital billing department or patient financial services office. Ask specifically to apply for financial assistance or charity care — do not just ask about 'payment options,' as that conversation will default to payment plans.
Request the financial assistance application and a copy of the hospital's financial assistance policy (FAP). Under IRS 501(r), nonprofit hospitals must provide both on request.

Complete the application in full. Missing information is the most common reason for delays or denials — attach all required documentation (tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements, and documentation of extraordinary expenses).

Submit the application before the deadline. Most hospitals require applications within 240 days of the date of service, which is the ACA minimum. Some hospitals have shorter windows, so confirm the deadline when you request the application.
Get confirmation in writing that the application has been received and is under review. Ask for a reference number and the name of the person handling your application.
Do not make payments on the account while the application is pending. Payment can be treated as acceptance of the full billed amount and may complicate your application.
Follow up in writing if you don't receive a determination within the timeframe the hospital specified.

content.text: If you were not informed about financial assistance options during or after your visit, that may itself be a violation of 501(r) requirements. The regulations require hospitals to make 'reasonable efforts' to determine whether patients qualify for financial assistance and to notify patients about the availability of those programs. If you received no information about charity care, mention that to the billing office — it sometimes creates additional willingness to accommodate.

links.text: How Hospital Charity Care Programs Work
Proving Financial Hardship to Get Your Medical Bill Reduced
Medical Debt Forgiveness Programs That Can Erase Your Balance

content.text: What 'Hardship' Means in Hospital Policy vs. IRS Rules
The term 'hardship' is used in two distinct contexts in the medical billing world, and understanding the difference matters when you're trying to get a medical bill waived.

In hospital policy, 'hardship' typically refers to a financial situation that makes it genuinely difficult to pay a medical bill, even if your income technically exceeds the standard charity care thresholds. Most hospitals have a separate hardship waiver program — sometimes called an 'extraordinary circumstances' policy or 'financial hardship exception' — that considers factors beyond raw income. A patient earning $70,000 a year who has just been diagnosed with cancer, has a spouse with a separate serious illness, is managing $3,000 per month in other medical expenses, or has experienced a sudden job loss may qualify for a hardship waiver even if they don't meet the income threshold for standard charity care. Hardship is about your ability to pay relative to your specific circumstances, not just your gross income.

In IRS rules, 'hardship' has a different and more specific meaning when it appears in the 501(r) context. The IRS requires that nonprofit hospitals not engage in extraordinary collection actions (lawsuits, liens, wage garnishment, reporting to credit bureaus) against a patient without first making reasonable efforts to determine whether they qualify for financial assistance. There are also IRS rules around how hospitals must handle accounts in arrears. But the IRS does not define a universal income threshold for 'hardship' — that determination is left to each hospital's individual financial assistance policy, subject to the ACA requirement that the policy be consistently applied.

What this means practically: if the hospital says you don't qualify for standard charity care, explicitly ask about the hardship waiver process or the extraordinary circumstances exception. Provide documentation of the specific circumstances that make payment difficult even at your income level. A well-documented hardship case — especially one that demonstrates the bill represents more than a set percentage of your annual income (many hospitals use 10% of annual income as a hardship threshold) — significantly improves your chances of getting a medical bill waived or substantially reduced.
The Prompt-Pay Discount Strategy

Even if you earn too much to qualify for charity care, the prompt-pay discount is one of the most reliable ways to get a medical bill reduced significantly. Most hospitals offer discounts of 10–30% for patients who can pay a settled amount within 30 days of billing. Some hospital systems offer prompt-pay discounts of up to 40% for uninsured patients who pay in full immediately.

The key to maximizing the prompt-pay strategy is combining it with a lump-sum settlement offer rather than accepting the billed amount minus a percentage. Instead of saying 'I'll pay today if you give me 30% off,' try: 'I'm prepared to pay a lump sum today to close this account. Based on what Medicare would reimburse for these services, I'm prepared to offer [X amount]. Can we close this account today for that amount?' This approach frames the negotiation around fair market value rather than a simple percentage of a potentially inflated chargemaster rate.

To find the Medicare reimbursement rate for your specific procedures, use the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) physician fee schedule lookup tool available at cms.gov. Medicare rates are publicly available for nearly every procedure code. Insurance companies typically pay providers 130–160% of Medicare rates. If a hospital is billing you at 500% of Medicare's rate, presenting the Medicare rate as a reference point for your settlement offer is a powerful negotiating tool.
Using Competing Quotes to Negotiate

Hospital Price Transparency regulations, which took full effect in January 2021 under rules finalized by CMS, require all U.S. hospitals to publish their standard charges — including negotiated insurance rates — in machine-readable files and to post shoppable service prices in a consumer-friendly format. This regulation created a powerful tool for patients: the ability to know what competitors charge for the same services.

If your local hospital is billing you $8,000 for a procedure and a competitor hospital 10 miles away publishes a price of $3,200 for the same procedure code, you have a compelling data point. Present the competitor's published price when negotiating. Hospitals are not legally required to match a competitor's price, but presenting concrete market data shifts the negotiation significantly in your favor. The billing department knows their chargemaster rates are inflated relative to what insurers actually pay; a patient who presents documented market pricing signals sophistication that typically produces a more serious negotiating response.

links.text: How to Negotiate Medical Bills: The Complete Guide
How to Negotiate Medical Bills Without Insurance

content.text: What to Say When the Hospital Says No

A denial is almost never the final answer. Hospital billing departments are staffed by individuals who have approval authority up to a certain level — they may be telling you no because they genuinely lack the authority to approve what you're asking, not because it's impossible. Here is a structured approach to responding to a 'no.'

First response: 'Can you tell me specifically why I don't qualify, and can I have that in writing?' A specific reason gives you something to address. A denial for incomplete documentation is easily remedied. A denial because your income exceeds the threshold may be appealable if you have extraordinary circumstances.

Second response: 'I'd like to speak with a supervisor in patient financial services and submit a formal appeal.' Every nonprofit hospital's 501(r)-compliant financial assistance policy must include an appeals process. Using it signals that you are not going away, which alone sometimes produces a different outcome from a different person with more authority.

Third response: 'I understand I may not qualify for charity care, but I'd like to discuss a hardship waiver and a lump-sum settlement. This bill represents [X%] of my annual income, which I believe meets your hardship criteria. I'm prepared to close this account today for [Y amount].' Combining hardship framing with a concrete settlement offer addresses two different approval pathways simultaneously.

Fourth response: 'I'm aware that your hospital is required to provide financial assistance under IRS Section 501(r). I want to make sure this account is properly reviewed before any collection activity begins.' Explicitly citing the legal requirement — calmly, not combatively — changes the tone of the conversation and often escalates your case to someone with more authority to help.
Specific Tactics for Particularly High Bills

For medical bills over $10,000, the waiver and reduction strategies above still apply, but they're worth supplementing with a few additional tactics. First, get the itemized bill reviewed for errors before negotiating the balance. Studies consistently show that 30–80% of medical bills contain errors, and catching a duplicate charge or an upcoding error can reduce the bill significantly before any assistance programs are even applied.

Second, understand whether the bill includes charges from multiple providers — hospitals and physicians often bill separately, and your leverage and options are different with each. A hospital may have a robust charity care program while the attending physician's billing service operates independently. Apply the same charity care inquiry to each provider separately.

Third, for very large bills — $50,000 or more — consider engaging a professional medical billing advocate. The contingency fees they charge (typically 25–35% of savings) are justified when the savings themselves are substantial, and experienced advocates often achieve outcomes that patients negotiating on their own do not. For bills in the $5,000–$50,000 range, the strategies in this guide and a systematic approach to the processes described here are usually sufficient to achieve meaningful reductions without professional help.

links.text: How to Get an Itemized Medical Bill
Common Hospital Billing Errors and How to Catch Them

content.text: State-Level Charity Care Laws Add Additional Protections

Federal 501(r) requirements set a floor, but many states have enacted additional charity care protections that are more generous. California's Hospital Fair Pricing Act, for example, requires hospitals to offer free care to patients at or below 350% FPL and prohibits hospitals from billing more than the Medicare rate to uninsured patients with income below 350% FPL. New York, New Jersey, Illinois, and Washington have similar state-level protections.

These state laws sometimes apply to for-profit hospitals that aren't subject to federal 501(r) rules. They may also have different application deadlines, different documentation requirements, and in some cases, retroactive coverage for bills already paid. Looking up your state's specific charity care laws — your state attorney general's website or your state's department of health is usually the best source — can reveal additional protections beyond what the hospital's standard financial assistance policy offers.
